metadata.career_relevance: The fastest query is one you never make. Flipkart's product page was hitting the DB for every page view (200M/day). Adding a two-level cache (Caffeine L1 + Redis L2) reduced DB load by 95% — same infrastructure handled 20× more traffic. After this topic, you can design a correct caching strategy that doesn't get stale or cause stampedes.

hook.real_world_problem:
Flipkart product page: 200M page views per day. Without cache: 200M DB queries/day.

```
DB queries/day: 200,000,000
Avg query time: 5ms
Total DB time: 1,000,000 seconds/day → 278 hours/day on a 1-server DB!

With L1 (Caffeine) + L2 (Redis):
  L1 hit rate: 40% → 80M DB queries avoided (in-process, ~0.1ms)
  L2 hit rate: 55% → 110M Redis hits avoided (network, ~1ms)
  DB hits:  5% → 10M queries/day (5× reduction from just L2)
  Total time: 5M seconds/day → 94% reduction!
```
hook.what_you_will_build: Two-level cache: Caffeine (L1, in-process) → Redis (L2, distributed) → DB. Cache-aside pattern with hit rate metrics and stampede protection.

visual_aid.description: Cache hierarchy: Request → L1 Caffeine (in-process, 30s TTL, 1000 entries) → L1 hit? return (0.1ms). L1 miss → L2 Redis (shared, 5min TTL) → L2 hit? populate L1 + return (1ms). L2 miss → DB (5ms) → populate Redis + Caffeine + return. Metrics panel showing hit rate by level: L1=40%, L2=55%, DB=5%. Right panel: stampede protection with mutex on cache-miss.
visual_aid.alt_text: Two-level cache hierarchy: Caffeine L1 → Redis L2 → PostgreSQL DB with hit rate metrics

worked_example.scenario: Flipkart product lookup: L1 Caffeine (30s TTL, 1000 entries) → L2 Redis (5min TTL) → DB.
worked_example.problem_statement:
1. Cache-aside with two levels
2. Hit rate metrics (Counter by level)
3. @SpringBootTest: verify L1 hit on second call, L2 miss on first

step_by_step_solution.details: L1 (Caffeine): check first → L2 (Redis): check if L1 miss → DB: check if L2 miss → populate both caches on DB hit

worked_example.final_code:
// L1 TTL short (30s): buffer for L2 — instances don't need to coordinate
// L2 TTL longer (5min): shared source of truth across all instances
worked_example.expected_output:
# First call: l1.miss → l2.miss → db.hit → populate l2 + l1
# Second call: l1.hit (within 30s) → return immediately
# After 30s + within 5min: l1.miss → l2.hit → populate l1 → return
things_to_notice: L1 TTL shorter than L2: ensures L1 stays fresh by re-fetching from L2. L1=30s, L2=5min. After 30s, L1 misses → fetches from Redis (still fresh) → no DB hit needed.

Stampede protection: when a hot key expires, hundreds of requests simultaneously hit the DB. Fix: use `redis.opsForValue().setIfAbsent(lockKey, '1', 10s)` as a distributed lock, let only the first request fetch from DB.
Cache invalidation on write: when a product is updated, delete from both L1 and L2 before the new value is written. Or use write-through: write to cache AND DB simultaneously.
maximumSize(1000): Caffeine evicts LRU entries when full. In a payment service: cache the top 1000 merchant configs (accessed most frequently). 1001st merchant: evicts the least recently used.

industry_examples.use_case: L1 Caffeine (1000 products, 30s TTL) + L2 Redis (100K products, 5min TTL). L1 hit rate: 40% (popular products accessed many times per minute). L2 hit rate: 55%. DB hit rate: 5%. Result: 95% reduction in DB query count on 200M page views/day.

applications.interview_relevance: 'Design a caching strategy for a product catalog.' → Two-level cache: L1 Caffeine (in-process, small, short TTL) → L2 Redis (distributed, larger, longer TTL) → DB. Cache-aside pattern. Hit rate metrics (counter by level). Invalidate on write. Handle stampede with distributed lock on cache miss.
applications.real_world_workflow: Cache strategy: identify read-heavy, rarely-changing data (product details, merchant configs, exchange rates). Set TTL based on acceptable staleness. Monitor hit rate: < 80% L2 hit rate = either cache too small or TTL too short.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Not handling cache stampede: hot key expires, 1000 concurrent requests all miss and hit DB simultaneously.
common_gaps.remediation: On cache miss for a hot key: use a distributed lock (Redis SETNX). Only one request fetches from DB; others wait for cache to be populated, then read from cache.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: DB CPU spike every TTL interval; thousands of identical slow queries in pg_stat_statements exactly at cache expiry time

key_takeaways: L1 (Caffeine): in-process, microsecond access, bounded by JVM heap
L2 (Redis): distributed, millisecond access, shared across instances
Cache-aside: check L1 → L2 → DB; populate on miss
L1 TTL < L2 TTL: L1 is a buffer — stays fresh by re-fetching from L2
Hit rate metric: counter by level → Prometheus → Grafana
Stampede: SETNX distributed lock on hot key expiry
Cache invalidation: delete L1 + L2 before writing new value to DB

self_check_questions.question: Why should L1 (Caffeine) TTL be shorter than L2 (Redis) TTL?
self_check_questions.answer: L1 is a local, in-process cache — it's not shared between service instances. When Instance A updates a product, Instance B's L1 still has the old value. Short L1 TTL (30s) ensures the stale data is evicted quickly. When L1 misses, Instance B fetches from L2 (Redis), which was already updated (or invalidated) when Instance A wrote. L2 TTL (5min) can be longer because Redis is shared — one invalidation reaches all instances. If L1 had the same TTL as L2 (5min), Instance B could serve stale product data for up to 5 minutes after an update.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Final Module 22 topic: Load Testing with Gatling — verifying your optimized service actually meets SLOs under realistic production load. Test: 1000 users, 2 minutes, p99 < 200ms. Validate: no service degradation under sustained traffic.
